title: Beyond Air Expands Commercial Access for LungFit® PH Through a Purchasing Agreement with a Top 3 National Group Purchasing Organization (GPO)
content:
Beyond Air™

GARDEN CITY, N.Y., April 01, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Beyond Air, Inc. (NASDAQ: XAIR), a commercial-stage medical device and biopharmaceutical company focused on harnessing the power of nitric oxide (NO) to improve patients’ lives, today announced it has been awarded a national group purchasing agreement for inhaled nitric oxide therapy with a leading U.S. group purchasing organization (GPO). Effective April 1, 2026, the agreement allows participating member hospitals and health systems to access the LungFit® PH system.

This new agreement builds on Beyond Air’s existing relationships with leading U.S. GPOs, including Vizient and Premier, Inc., further expanding access to a broad network of healthcare providers in the United States.

“Our GPO relationships play an important role in expanding access to LungFit PH, and this additional agreement meaningfully increases our reach by nearly 2,000 U.S. hospitals and health systems,” said Robert Goodman, Chief Executive Officer of Beyond Air. “By establishing purchasing agreements with three of the largest U.S.-based GPOs, we have created a more streamlined pathway for providers to evaluate and adopt a tankless nitric oxide solution. The endorsement of these GPOs not only expands our commercial reach, it also helps accelerate adoption as hospitals seek more efficient, cost-effective alternatives to traditional nitric oxide delivery systems.”

The LungFit PH platform utilizes patented Plasma Pulse Technology® to generate nitric oxide on demand from room air. By eliminating the need for traditional source supplies, LungFit PH supports simplified clinical workflows, reduced supply chain burden, and hospital sustainability initiatives.

This latest GPO agreement reinforces Beyond Air’s continued momentum in expanding access to LungFit PH through leading group purchasing organizations and supporting hospitals in delivering high-quality, cost-effective care.

About Beyond Air, Inc.

Beyond Air is a commercial-stage medical device and biopharmaceutical company dedicated to harnessing the power of endogenous and exogenous nitric oxide (NO) to improve the lives of patients suffering from respiratory illnesses, neurological disorders, and solid tumors. The Company has received FDA approval and CE Mark for its first system, LungFit PH, for the treatment of term and near-term neonates with hypoxic respiratory failure. For more information, visit www.beyondair.net.

Forward-Looking Statements

This press release contains “forward-looking statements” within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. These statements include, but are not limited to, statements regarding the Company’s expectations related to the commercial growth, market adoption of LungFit PH, expansion in the U.S. and international markets, and the Company’s long-term strategic and financial performance. Forward-looking statements may be identified by words such as “anticipate,” “believe,” “expect,” “intend,” “plan,” “potential,” “will,” “would,” “could,” “may,” and similar expressions, or by the use of future tense. These statements are based on current expectations, estimates, forecasts, and projections, as well as the beliefs and assumptions of management.

Story Continues

Because forward-looking statements relate to future events, they are subject to inherent risks and uncertainties, many of which are beyond the Company’s control, that could cause actual results to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. These risks and uncertainties include, but are not limited to, risks related to the Company’s ability to successfully execute its commercial strategy, achieve market adoption of its products, maintain and expand customer relationships, manage leadership transitions effectively, obtain additional financing, and other risks described in the “Risk Factors” section of Beyond Air, Inc.’s most recent Annual Report on Form 10-K and subsequent filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

title: Nanosonics awarded Specialty High Level Ultrasound Probe Disinfection Equipment agreement with Premier, Inc.
content:
INDIANAPOLIS, March 2, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Nanosonics, Inc. has been awarded a national group purchasing agreement for Specialty High Level Ultrasound Probe Disinfection Equipment with Premier, Inc. Effective March 1, 2026, the new agreement allows Premier members, at their discretion, to take advantage of special pricing and terms pre-negotiated by Premier for the trophon®3 device, an automated high-level disinfection system for ultrasound probes.Nanosonics, Inc. Infection Prevention For Life. (PRNewsfoto/Nanosonics, Inc.)

"The launch of Nanosonics' Premier agreement provides these clinicians with the confidence to care, meeting patient safety and compliance standards for ultrasound high-level disinfection," said Bill Haydon, Nanosonics President of North America. "As the industry leader in ultrasound high-level disinfection, this contract streamlines access to our proven, evidence-backed trophon technology to thousands of healthcare facilities and systems."

Nanosonics' trophon technology revolutionized ultrasound high-level disinfection by introducing a fully automated process, creating workflow efficiencies and measurable improvements to compliance. With the broadest probe compatibility and demonstrated reliability, many facilities have standardized on this technology, leading to a global install base of over 35,000 devices. Our latest innovation, the trophon3 device further strengthens system wide compliance with built-in traceability and connectivity integration options for documentation.

Premier, Inc. is a leading technology-driven healthcare improvement company. Playing a critical role in the rapidly evolving healthcare industry, Premier unites providers, suppliers and payers to make healthcare better with national scale, smarter with actionable intelligence and faster with novel technologies. Headquartered in Charlotte, N.C., Premier offers integrated data and analytics, collaboratives, supply chain solutions, advisory services and other solutions in service of our mission to improve the health of communities. Please visit Premier's news site at www.premierinc.com; as well as X, Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Instagram and Premier's blog for more information about the company.

About Nanosonics Nanosonics, Inc. is an Australian infection prevention company that has successfully developed and commercialized a unique automated disinfection technology, the trophon® device, representing the first major innovation in high-level disinfection for ultrasound probes in more than 25 years. The trophon device is fast becoming the global standard of care for ultrasound probe disinfection.

Story Continues

title: Inmar Intelligence awarded renewed Pharmacy Reverse Distribution agreement with Premier, Inc.
content:
WINSTON-SALEM, N.C., Feb. 19, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Inmar Intelligence has been awarded a renewed national group purchasing agreement for Pharmacy Reverse Distribution Services with Premier, Inc., extending a long-standing relationship between the organizations. Effective February 1, 2026, the renewed agreement allows Premier members, at their discretion, to continue taking advantage of special pricing and terms pre-negotiated by Premier for pharmacy reverse distribution services.Inmar Logo (PRNewsfoto/Inmar Intelligence)

"Premier members continue to choose Inmar because of the scale, reliability and value we deliver across their pharmacy operations," said Anthony Garcia, Vice President, Healthcare Sales at Inmar Intelligence. "This is a direct reflection of the trust placed in our ability to recover value, maintain regulatory compliance, improve performance and simplify increasingly complex workflows. This renewed agreement reinforces our shared focus on helping members improve financial performance while operating with clarity and confidence."

Inmar provides pharmacy reverse distribution services designed to simplify returns, strengthen compliance and improve financial recovery. As pharmacy operations face increasing regulatory requirements, cost pressures and inventory complexity, reverse distribution has become a critical component of financial and compliance performance.

Inmar delivers 99% processing accuracy across all pharmaceutical returns channels and serves more than 50,000 pharmacies nationwide. Its vertically integrated, one-touch reverse distribution model accelerates credit recovery, reduces third-party handling and improves visibility and control across returned pharmaceutical inventory. By bringing returns, recalls, compliance and disposal into a single integrated solution, Inmar helps pharmacies support consistent, auditable processes while minimizing operational risk.

Premier is a leading technology-driven healthcare improvement company. Playing a critical role in the rapidly evolving healthcare industry, Premier unites providers, suppliers and payers to make healthcare better with national scale, smarter with actionable intelligence and faster with novel technologies. Headquartered in Charlotte, N.C., Premier offers integrated data and analytics, collaboratives, supply chain solutions, advisory services and other solutions in service of its mission to improve the health of communities.

About Inmar Intelligence Through curiosity and the intelligent use of data and technology, we optimize the healthcare value chain to improve patient safety, access and affordability. As a partner for over 45 years to health systems, pharmacies and life sciences companies, we design and build solutions to address the complex challenges in the business of healthcare. Our mission is to enable improved financial and patient outcomes to meet the evolving needs of the healthcare industry. For more information about Inmar Intelligence, visit inmar.com and follow us on LinkedIn, or email us at solutions@inmar.com.

Story Continues

title: Andor Health, the Provider of Agentic AI Software Infrastructure for Healthcare, Awarded National Group Purchasing Agreements with Premier, Inc.
content:
ORLANDO, Fla., Feb. 12, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Andor Health, the leader in agentic AI solutions for healthcare, has been awarded three national group purchasing agreements with Premier Inc. The agreements are in the categories of Virtual Care, Clinical Communications & Collaboration, and Remote Patient Monitoring. ThinkAndor®, Andor Health's agentic AI-powered infrastructure, modernizes virtual care workflows, enables remote patient monitoring, and transforms clinical communications across the care continuum.(PRNewsfoto/Andor Health)

Effective Date Category Virtual Care December 1, 2025 Clinical Communications & Collaboration February 1, 2026 Remote Patient Monitoring March 1, 2026

The new agreements allow Premier members, at their discretion, to take advantage of special pricing and terms pre-negotiated by Premier for ThinkAndor®. As the #1 agentic AI software infrastructure for healthcare™, ThinkAndor® brings agentic intelligence to optimize workflows, reduce administrative burdens, and improve system-wide operational performance.

"Being selected by Premier for the Virtual Care Category, as the only agentic AI platform spanning all virtual experiences, including virtual visits, virtual nursing & observation, virtual inpatient consultations, clinical communications & collaboration, and remote patient monitoring, is both a tremendous honor and a powerful validation of our mission," said Raj Toleti, Chairman & CEO of Andor Health. "As the #1 agentic AI software infrastructure for healthcare™, we are committed to modernizing care delivery by leveraging AI to enhance workflows that elevate clinician experience, improve patient engagement, drive tangible ROI, and enable truly transformative operational efficiency."

Premier is a leading technology-driven healthcare improvement company. Playing a critical role in the rapidly evolving healthcare industry, Premier unites providers, suppliers and payers to make healthcare better with national scale, smarter with actionable intelligence and faster with novel technologies. Headquartered in Charlotte, N.C., Premier offers integrated data and analytics, collaboratives, supply chain solutions, advisory services and other solutions in service of our mission to improve the health of communities.

About Andor Health

Andor Health revolutionizes clinical care through generative AI-powered virtual care experiences across the care continuum. Our virtual care experiences empower healthcare providers with actionable insights & ambient monitoring to deliver care at scale, with proven return on investment. ThinkAndor®, the #1 healthcare AI software infrastructure for healthcare, leverages AI agents to harness generative AI unlocking data stored in various systems, including electronic medical records, biometric devices, & other signals to deliver actionable intelligence in real time to care teams. By optimizing communication workflows, ThinkAndor® accelerates time to treatment, decreases clinician burnout, and drives better patient outcomes.

Story Continues

For more information, visit Andor Health or follow us on LinkedIn and X.

title: AOP Health US, LLC Awarded Pharmaceutical Agreement With Premier, Inc.
content:
intensive care ward; photo credit: AdobeStock_1875570500

WILMINGTON, Del., February 10, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--AOP Health US, LLC has been awarded a Pharmaceutical national group purchasing agreement with Premier, Inc. Effective August 1, 2025, the agreement allows Premier members, at their discretion, to take advantage of special pricing and terms pre-negotiated by Premier for Rapiblyk® (landiolol).

"This represents an important milestone in ensuring that Rapiblyk® availability is secured nationwide, supporting clinicians and healthcare providers in critical care environments," said John Kimmet,General Manager of AOP Health US. "We’re reinforcing our commitment to making Rapiblyk® accessible where it’s needed most."

Rapiblyk® (landiolol) is an ultra-short-acting, selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the short-term reduction of ventricular rate in adults with supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) or irregular rapid heart rate, including atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter, in critical care settings.

Premier is a leading technology-driven healthcare improvement company. Playing a critical role in the rapidly evolving healthcare industry, Premier unites providers, suppliers and payers to make healthcare better with national scale, smarter with actionable intelligence and faster with novel technologies. Headquartered in Charlotte, N.C., Premier offers integrated data and analytics, collaboratives, supply chain solutions, advisory services and other solutions in service of our mission to improve the health of communities.

About Rapiblyk®

Rapiblyk® (landiolol) is an ultra-short-acting, selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker indicated for the short-term reduction of ventricular rate in adults with supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) or irregular rapid heart rate, including atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter, especially in perioperative, postoperative, or critical-care settings. Rapiblyk® provides rapid onset and offset of action, allowing clinicians to achieve precise, controlled rate management with a favorable hemodynamic profile.

The product is supplied as an intravenous infusion designed for use in a monitored hospital setting.

INDICATION

Rapiblyk® is indicated for the short-term reduction of ventricular rate in adults with supraventricular tachycardia, including atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter.

IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION

CONTRAINDICATIONS

Rapiblyk® is contraindicated in patients with:

Severe sinus bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, or >1st-degree AV block Decompensated heart failure Cardiogenic shock Pulmonary hypertension Known hypersensitivity to landiolol or formulation components

Story Continues

WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS

Includes risk of hypotension, bradycardia, cardiac failure, bronchospasm, masking of hypoglycemia symptoms, infusion site reactions, worsening of Prinzmetal’s angina, complications in pheochromocytoma, peripheral vascular disease, abrupt withdrawal effects, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and unresponsiveness to epinephrine in hypersensitivity reactions.

ADVERSE REACTIONS

Most important and common adverse reaction is hypotension which in clinical trials occurred in 9.9% of patients receiving RAPIBLYK vs. 1% in those receiving placebo. Please consult the full Prescribing Information for Rapiblyk®: https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2024/217202s000lbl.pdf

About AOP Health

AOP Health is a global enterprise with roots in Austria and a mission to address unmet medical needs through innovative, science-driven therapies in cardiovascular and rare disease. Since 1996, the AOP Health Group has been recognized as a pioneer in developing integrated therapy solutions that combine deep scientific expertise with a strong commitment to patient and physician needs.

Building on this foundation, AOP Health US, LLC was established in 2025 to expand the company’s impact in the United States. The team is focused on introducing its 2024 US FDA approved product for use in the critical care setting, while building the infrastructure to support healthcare professionals and the patients they serve.

Rapiblyk® is a USPTO registered trademark of AOP Orphan Pharmaceuticals GmbH (Austria).

© 2026 AOP Health. All rights reserved.

title: Convatec awarded Enterostomal Therapy Products agreements with Premier, Inc.
content:
LONDON & BOSTON, February 09, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Convatec, a global medical products and technologies company focused on solutions for the management of chronic conditions, has been awarded two group purchasing agreements in the Enterostomal Therapy Products category with Premier, Inc., effective April 1, 2026:

National Agreement: The new agreement allows Premier members, at their discretion, to take advantage of special pricing and terms pre-negotiated by Premier for enterostomal therapy products. AscenDrive® Agreement: Convatec has also been awarded a group purchasing agreement for enterostomal therapy products with Premier’s AscenDrive® program, which is designed to drive the highest-level commitment and savings for members through aggregated purchasing of high-quality products and services. The program brings together Premier’s most committed members to coordinate purchasing decisions and maintain standardization across their facilities. By providing additional value to Premier contracted suppliers through volume and commitment, the program is expected to achieve best-in-market pricing.

"At Convatec, our forever caring promise drives everything we do. Through these new agreements with Premier, we’re making it easier for providers to access our trusted medical solutions for people living with an ostomy, including Convatec‘s dedicated me+ support program. Together, these agreements strengthen our focus on delivering high-quality ostomy solutions while providing Premier members with exceptional value and savings. We remain committed to helping people live confidently and improve health outcomes." - Dan Yrigoyen, Vice President - US, Ostomy Care at Convatec.

Premier is a leading technology-driven healthcare improvement company. Playing a critical role in the rapidly evolving healthcare industry, Premier unites providers, suppliers and payers to make healthcare better with national scale, smarter with actionable intelligence and faster with novel technologies. Headquartered in Charlotte, N.C., Premier offers integrated data and analytics, collaboratives, supply chain solutions, advisory services and other solutions in service of our mission to improve the health of communities.

About Convatec

Pioneering trusted medical solutions to improve the lives we touch: Convatec is a global medical products and technologies company, focused on solutions for the management of chronic conditions, with leading positions in Advanced Wound Care, Ostomy Care, Continence Care, and Infusion Care. With more than 10,000 colleagues, we provide products and services in around 90 countries, united by a promise to be forever caring. Our solutions provide a range of benefits, from infection prevention, treatment for hard to heal wounds, at-risk skin and ulcerated tissue to supporting debilitating conditions, improved patient outcomes and reduced care costs. Convatec's revenues in 2024 were over $2 billion. The company is a constituent of the FTSE 100 Index (LSE:CTEC). To learn more please visit http://www.convatecgroup.com.

title: R1 Appoints Andy Brailo as Chief Commercial Officer
content:
R1 RCM Holdco Inc.

CHICAGO, Feb. 06, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- R1 (“R1” or the “Company”), the leader in healthcare revenue management, today announced that Andy Brailo has joined the Company as Chief Commercial Officer. In this role, Mr. Brailo will oversee R1’s growth and revenue strategy and is expected to scale commercial capabilities, expand its customer base and enhance go-to-market execution to meet increasing demand for R1’s AI-enabled solutions.

Mr. Brailo brings deep expertise across sales, training and development, operations, process management and customer service delivery. He previously spent 25 years at Premier Inc., where he most recently served as Chief Commercial Officer. At Premier, he successfully partnered with members, suppliers and staff to develop and design customized and scalable solutions to help healthcare providers improve quality and reduce costs in the evolving healthcare industry.

“I am pleased to welcome Andy to R1 as we scale our commercial capabilities to match the pace of our growth,” said Joe Flanagan, Chief Executive Officer of R1. “Andy brings extensive commercial and healthcare market experience, and I look forward to benefitting from his expertise. I’m confident he will help us build on our momentum with the Phare Operating System – bringing together agentic technology and deep revenue cycle expertise to meet our customers where they are and shape the future of revenue performance.”

“I have long admired R1’s ability to pair innovative technology with a deep understanding of healthcare provider needs,” said Mr. Brailo. “For decades, the operating margins of healthcare providers have been stressed. R1’s innovative revenue operating system drives improved financial and operational performance through a complete ecosystem of solutions scaled for the customer’s needs, allowing them to do what they do best – focus on their mission, the communities and their patients.”

About R1

R1 is the leader in healthcare revenue management, helping providers achieve new levels of performance through smart orchestration. A pioneer in the industry, R1 created the first Healthcare Revenue Operating System: a modular, intelligent platform that integrates automation, AI, and human expertise to strengthen the entire revenue cycle. With more than 20 years of experience, R1 partners with 1,000 providers, including 95 of the top 100 U.S. health systems, and handles over 270 million payer transactions annually. This scale provides unmatched operational insight to help healthcare organizations unlock greater long-term value. To learn more, visit: https://www.r1rcm.com.

Story Continues

title: When Scale Becomes a Tollbooth: The Economics Inside Premier
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

The Place Where the Economics Settle

In practical terms, Premier sits inside the operating rhythm of a hospital long before a patient is treated. Its platform helps hospitals source everyday medical supplies, from gloves, syringes, and surgical kits to imaging equipment, implants, and certain pharmaceutical categories, under pre-negotiated contracts. Beyond purchasing, Premier aggregates usage data across its member base, allowing hospitals to benchmark costs, standardize products, and manage compliance with clinical and regulatory protocols. If Premier's platform were unavailable for a day, a hospital would still function, surgeries would not stop, but procurement teams would lose pricing visibility, contract access, and data-driven guidance, forcing them to revert to fragmented supplier relationships and manual decision-making. Premier's value lies not in moving boxes or distributing drugs, but in quietly shaping how hospitals decide what to buy, from whom, and at what price.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 8 Warning Signs with PINC. Is PINC fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

This role is distinct from logistics-focused platforms such as Amazon Business, which emphasizes fulfillment and speed, or drug distributors like McKesson, Cardinal Health, and AmerisourceBergen, which physically move pharmaceuticals through the supply chain. Premier does not own inventory, operate warehouses, or compete on delivery. Its economics stem from coordination, data, and contract leverage, an upstream layer that influences purchasing behavior rather than executing it.

The company operates between hospitals trying to control costs and suppliers trying to preserve pricing power. It is not a healthcare provider, and it is not a manufacturer. Its role is structural: coordinating purchasing, standardizing contracts, and embedding data into how hospitals decide what to buy and from whom. Over time, that position has produced a business that looks unusually capital-light for healthcare and generates steady cash without relying on volume growth. But this position also puts Premier in the line of fire. Any company that influences pricing in healthcare inevitably attracts scrutiny, political, regulatory, and reputational. That tension has shaped how investors view the stock. At times, Premier is treated as a durable platform with embedded economics; at others, as a middle layer vulnerable to pressure from both sides.

What makes the current moment worth examining is not a change in Premier's products, but a shift in how exposed those economics are. Hospitals are emerging from a period of acute stress marked by labor inflation and reimbursement pressure. Suppliers are pushing harder on margins. At the same time, Premier has simplified its portfolio, narrowed its focus, and leaned more heavily into its core platform. That combination forces a more honest assessment of what the company really earns and why.

Story Continues

The question is not whether Premier is a good business in the abstract. It is whether its position behaves more like a negotiated toll, durable but constrained, or a discretionary service whose economics fluctuate with sentiment and policy. The answer determines whether today's valuation reflects caution or misunderstanding

How the Platform Turns Participation Into Cash

Premier's economics are easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a services company and start thinking about it as a coordination layer. Hospitals do not join Premier to buy analytics dashboards or consulting hours. They join because coordinating purchasing across thousands of products is costly, fragmented, and operationally distracting. Premier absorbs that complexity. In return, it earns fees tied to contract participation and supplier relationships, revenue streams that scale with engagement rather than with physical throughput.

This structure explains two things that often get conflated. First, why Premier can produce meaningful free cash flow without heavy capital investment. Once contracts are in place and data systems are integrated, incremental participation does not require proportional spending. Second, why growth has never been the point. The business does not need expanding hospital counts or aggressive price increases to remain economically viable; it needs retention and relevance.

That relevance has been tested repeatedly. Hospital margins have been under pressure from labor inflation, reimbursement lag, and regulatory scrutiny. In that environment, Premier's value proposition becomes less about savings headlines and more about process stability. When budgets tighten, coordination matters more, not less. That dynamic helps explain why the company has historically held onto customers even during periods when its own revenue growth slowed.

At the same time, the model imposes a ceiling. Premier cannot extract unlimited economics without inviting resistance. Its role works best when it is perceived as aligned with hospitals rather than as a rent collector. That balance, earning enough to justify the platform while staying below the threshold that triggers pushback, defines the long-term cash profile of the business.

Recent portfolio decisions sharpen this trade-off. By simplifying its business mix and reducing exposure to lower-return activities, Premier has made its economics cleaner and easier to analyze. But it has also made the core model more visible. There is less optionality to hide behind, which means future results will increasingly reflect the true earning power of the platform itself.

For an owner, this is the critical transition point. The question is no longer whether Premier can generate cash, it clearly can. The question is how stable and repeatable that cash generation is once the business is stripped down to its essentials, and whether the market's current valuation already assumes that stability.

What the Cash Looks Like on a Per-Share Basis

Once Premier is stripped down to its core platform, the most revealing question is not how much cash the company generates in total, but how consistently that cash accrues to each share.

Over the past several years, Premier has produced steady free cash flow despite operating in one of the most pressured segments of healthcare. That resilience matters. Hospital customers have faced rising labor costs, reimbursement lag, and episodic financial stress, yet Premier's cash generation has remained intact. The platform has proven capable of sustaining itself through difficult operating environments.

What makes the per-share view important, however, is that Premier's economics are no longer expanding through breadth. The company is not meaningfully increasing the number of hospitals it serves, nor is it pushing aggressive volume growth. The cash story is now driven by stability and efficiency, not scale.

That shows up in the per-share data. Free cash flow per share has held up well, but it has not compounded rapidly. Share count has been relatively stable, which is a positive, there has been no meaningful dilution masking the results, yet the absence of dilution also means there is no optical lift. What shareholders see is close to the underlying reality of the business.

This matters because Premier's model does not naturally produce operating leverage in the way a software platform might. Incremental margins exist, but they are constrained by the company's role in the healthcare ecosystem. Premier earns its economics by facilitating coordination, not by extracting maximum value from each transaction. That makes the cash flow durable, but it also places a ceiling on how quickly it can grow on a per-share basis.

Recent portfolio actions reinforce that interpretation. By simplifying the business and focusing on its core platform, Premier has reduced noise in the numbers. That clarity is useful for owners, but it also removes the illusion of latent growth optionality. What remains is a business whose strength lies in repeatability rather than acceleration.

Premier's per-share cash generation is real and resilient, but it is also bounded. The investment case therefore depends less on future expansion and more on whether today's valuation already reflects that steady, utility-like cash profile, or whether the market is still discounting the platform more heavily than its durability warrants.

Capital Allocation

Premier's reported financial stability can obscure a more nuanced reality: the company does generate reliable cash, but how that cash is redeployed matters far more than the headline totals. Over the past several years, Premier has consistently produced free cash flow that exceeds net income, a sign of low capital intensity and disciplined working capital management. That is the easy part. The harder question is what happens next, whether those dollars are reinvested at attractive incremental returns or simply recycled to preserve the status quo.

Premier's capital allocation only looks passive if growth is assumed to be the objective. In reality, the company operates in a market that is structurally bounded. There are only so many hospitals, only so much purchasing volume, and limited scope for take-rate expansion without political or regulatory pushback. Within those constraints, aggressive reinvestment would likely destroy value rather than create it.

Management has responded to that reality by simplifying the portfolio and redirecting excess cash toward shareholders. Over the past several years, Premier has returned roughly $300 million annually through a combination of dividends and share repurchases, against a market capitalization of approximately $2.3 billion. That equates to a double-digit shareholder yield, a figure that matters far more in a mature, capital-light business than incremental revenue growth.

The buyback component is particularly important. At current valuations, repurchasing shares is not a neutral act. Each dollar spent reduces the share count meaningfully, lifting free cash flow and dividend capacity on a per-share basis even if total company cash flow remains flat. In other words, Premier does not need to grow to compound, it needs to persist. If the company were to continue allocating, for example, around $200 million per year to repurchases and $100 million to dividends, the arithmetic becomes straightforward. Per-share free cash flow would compound at a high-single- to low-double-digit rate, dividends per share would rise materially, and shareholders could see steady appreciation even without multiple expansion.

From an owner's perspective, this reframes Premier's strategy entirely. What might look like stagnation through a growth lens is, in fact, deliberate per-share compounding in a business that understands its own limits. In a politically constrained and operationally essential corner of healthcare, restraint is not a failure of imagination, it is a form of discipline.

The key takeaway is not that Premier allocates capital poorly. It does not. The more important point is that Premier allocates capital defensively, prioritizing stability over optionality. Whether that discipline creates value depends almost entirely on what an investor pays for those cash flows today.

Valuation

At today's price, Premier Inc. trades at a valuation that reflects durability, but also embeds modest expectations for per-share growth. To judge whether that pricing is attractive, it helps to compare Premier not to fast-growing healthcare disruptors, but to other capital-light, service-oriented businesses that investors might reasonably choose instead.

Company FCF Yield 5-yr FCF/Share CAGR Net Debt / EBITDA Business Character Premier ~56% ~34% Net cash / minimal Healthcare intermediary Evolent Health ~45% Mid-single digit ~1 Value-based care admin R1 RCM ~4% High single digit ~2 Outsourced hospital ops Veradigm ~56% Low single digit Net cash Healthcare data / software

What this comparison shows is that Premier is not obviously cheap, but also not aggressively priced. Its free cash flow yield is competitive, yet its per-share growth profile is at the lower end of the group. That gap matters.

Premier's economics are shaped by negotiated fees, volume stability, and long-term member relationships rather than operating leverage or pricing power. As a result, incremental capital does not compound at high marginal returns. Growth accrues slowly, and most of the owner's return comes from current cash generation, not reinvestment optionality.

Buying Premier today means underwriting something like:

a mid-single-digit free cash flow yield, plus low-to-mid single-digit per-share growth, assuming share count remains stable and margins hold.

That points to a high-single-digit total return, absent a change in business mix or capital allocation strategy. For investors seeking stability and balance-sheet safety in a volatile healthcare environment, that may be acceptable. For those looking to compound capital at materially higher rates, the opportunity cost becomes harder to ignore.

The market appears to understand this trade-off. Premier is priced as a steady allocator of cash, not as a business with a long reinvestment runway. Unless the company finds a way to meaningfully expand per-share economics, through higher-margin analytics, structural changes in supplier economics, or more aggressive capital returns, that valuation is likely to remain anchored.

Smart Money's Read on Premier

Institutional ownership in Premier Inc. reflects a very specific investor preference: predictability over reinvention.

Premier is primarily owned by managers who are comfortable underwriting steady cash flows, limited downside, and modest but reliable owner returns, rather than balance-sheet stress or transformational upside. This is not a stock that attracts deep-value activists or growth-oriented compounder funds. Instead, it sits comfortably in portfolios built around durability and capital preservation.

Funds such as O'Shaughnessy Asset Management and Ritholtz Wealth Management illustrate that orientation. These firms tend to favor businesses where outcomes are driven more by persistence than by timing or aggressive reinvestment. Premier fits that profile: recurring fees, long-standing member relationships, and a conservative balance sheet that prioritizes stability over expansion.

The shareholder base also includes systematic and multi-strategy firms, drawn less by a long-term growth narrative than by Premier's liquidity, low volatility, and resilient fundamentals. Their presence reinforces the idea that Premier functions well as a steady allocator of cash, not as a vehicle for outsized compounding.

What is most telling, however, is who does not dominate the register. Premier has not become a core holding for highly concentrated funds seeking long reinvestment runways and accelerating per-share economics. That absence aligns closely with the economic reality of the business itself. Premier generates dependable cash, but its intermediary role in healthcare places natural limits on how aggressively that cash can compound.

Taken together, the shareholder profile reinforces the broader investment case. Premier is owned by investors who value endurance, discipline, and downside control, not by those betting on structural growth or narrative-driven rerating. For a prospective owner, that alignment is informative. The appeal lies less in what the business might become and more in how consistently it does what it already does well.

Risks

The most important risk is economic compression, not volume loss. Premier sits between hospitals and suppliers, earning fees on negotiated purchasing and data services. That position is stable, but it also caps upside. As hospitals continue to face cost pressure and political scrutiny around healthcare pricing intensifies, Premier's ability to widen take rates or meaningfully expand margins is limited. The risk is not that the model breaks, but that it becomes more constrained, turning a mid-single-digit cash yield into the long-term ceiling rather than the starting point.

A second, closely related risk is disintermediation at the margin. Large health systems are increasingly sophisticated buyers, with internal procurement teams and direct supplier relationships. Premier's value proposition remains strong for smaller and mid-sized members, but if the largest systems continue to internalize analytics or negotiate directly with manufacturers, Premier could see gradual erosion in its most profitable relationships. This would likely show up slowly, through mix and pricing, rather than as a sudden revenue shock.

There is also a regulatory and reputational risk that is easy to overlook. Premier operates in a highly politicized environment where purchasing practices, data use, and supplier relationships can become subjects of public and regulatory attention. Changes in healthcare policy, antitrust enforcement, or reimbursement frameworks could alter the economics of group purchasing organizations in ways that are difficult to predict and hard to hedge. While Premier has navigated these waters for decades, the risk remains ever-present.

Finally, from an owner's perspective, there is a capital allocation risk of stagnation. Premier generates cash reliably, but its reinvestment options are narrow. If management continues to prioritize balance-sheet conservatism without finding higher-return uses for cash, shareholders may face a prolonged period where free cash flow is returned slowly and per-share growth remains modest. That outcome would not destroy value, but it would limit compounding in real terms, especially in a higher-rate world where opportunity cost matters.

None of these risks suggest fragility. Instead, they define the boundaries of the investment. Premier is resilient, but not elastic. For owners, understanding where those boundaries lie is essential, because the difference between a durable holding and a compelling one is often not the quality of the business, but the space it has left to grow.

Conclusion

Premier is not a story about acceleration. It is a story about positioning. The company sits at a quiet but powerful intersection of healthcare economics: embedded in hospital workflows, trusted as an intermediary, and paid for helping an overstretched system function more efficiently. That position explains the stability of its cash flows and the durability of its relationships. It also explains why growth is inherently bounded. Premier does not sell a product that scales explosively; it sells coordination, data, and purchasing leverage in a system that resists disruption.

For an owner, the question is therefore not whether Premier is a good business, it clearly is, but what kind of returns that business can reasonably deliver from here. At today's valuation, the market appears to be underwriting a steady, mid-single-digit compounding outcome: reliable free cash flow, modest per-share growth, and limited downside if the healthcare system remains intact. That is a defensible proposition, but not an open-ended one.

Premier earns its place as a capital-preserving compounder, not a reinvention story. Its value lies in predictability, not optionality. For investors who prioritize resilience, balance-sheet conservatism, and cash flow that survives cycles and policy shifts, that can be enough. For those seeking operating leverage or accelerating per-share growth, it likely is not.
